---
title: "Reference States, Heats of Mixing and Solution Reference Page<br/>*Reference Not Found: Mixing, Dissolving, and Changing the Rules*"
author: "DOFPro group"
format: html
html-math-method: mathjax
---
{{< include /LinkKey.qmd >}}
# Intro to *Reference Not Found: Mixing, Dissolving, and Changing the Rules*

Thermodynamic properties such as internal energy and enthalpy cannot usually be measured in an absolute sense. Instead, they are defined **relative to a reference state**.

Once a reference state is chosen, energy changes can be calculated consistently for processes such as mixing, dissolving, heating, or reacting. This video explains how reference states work and how they affect quantities such as heats of mixing and heats of solution.

## Reference Not Found: Mixing, Dissolving, and Changing the Rules

This video explains how thermodynamic reference states are defined and shows how they influence calculations involving mixing and dissolution processes.

<iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/ggU692PLWts?si=9RzEV4VOLBJSEnlV" title="YouTube video player" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen></iframe>

[Visuals](98-ReferenceStatesVIsuals.qmd)

# Examples and Definitions

## Examples Links

## Definitions

[Reference State](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard_state)  
: A specified state of a substance—usually defined by temperature, pressure, and phase—to which thermodynamic properties are referenced.  

For example, it is common to reference the internal energy of ice, liquid water, and steam to **liquid water at the triple point of water**, 273.16 K and 611.657 Pa.

[Heat or Enthalpy of Solution, $\Delta \hat{H}_s$](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enthalpy_change_of_solution)  
: The enthalpy change associated with dissolving a fixed amount of solute in a solvent at constant pressure, usually at constant temperature.

[Heat or Enthalpy of Mixing, $\Delta \hat{H}_m$](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enthalpy_of_mixing)  
: The enthalpy change associated with mixing fixed amounts of two or more substances at constant pressure, usually at constant temperature.

# Other Links and Videos

## Other Links

## Previous and Following Videos

### Two videos back

**Feeling the Heat? How to Get Your Best Estimate**<br/>*Estimating $C_p$, $\Delta \hat{H}_v$, $\Delta \hat{H}_m$*<br/>[Video](https://youtu.be/f_l3OFyuiPs), [Info Page](../../balances/energy/EstimatingHeatCapacitiesVideoInfoWebPage.qmd), [Visuals](../../balances/energy/95-EstimatingHeatCapacitiesVisuals.qmd)

### Previous video

**Dry Bulbs, Wet Bulbs, and My Will to Live: Mastering the Psych Chart**<br/>*Psychrometric Charts*<br/>[Video](https://youtu.be/Nz8ADo4X2Ss), [Info Page](../../balances/energy/PsychrometricChartsVideoInfoWebPage.qmd), [Visuals](../../balances/energy/97-PsychrometiricChartVIsuals.qmd)

### Next video

**The Isochronicles: This First Law Saga Is a Gas**<br/>*The 1^st^ Law for Ideal Gases for Isothermal, Isobaric, Isochoric, and Isentropic processes*<br/>[Video](https://youtu.be/iKsR3v1wu30), [Info Page](../../balances/energy/The1stLawForIdealGases.qmd), [Visuals](../../balances/energy/102-1stLawIdealGasesVisuals.qmd)

### Two videos forward

**Germain Hess and the Enthalpy of Doom**<br/>*Hess's Law and $\Delta \hat{H}^\circ_f$, $\Delta \hat{H}^\circ_r$, and $\Delta \hat{H}_c$*<br/>[Video](https://youtu.be/qdgH4v64vOQ), [Info Page](../../balances/energy/HeatsOfFormationAndReactionVideoInfoWebPage.qmd), [Visuals](../../balances/energy/104-HeatsOfReactionVisuals.qmd)
